title: Five tech giants are hiding $1.65tn in AI debt, using the trick that toppled Enron
excerpt: Look at what Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, and Oracle officially owe, and the numbers seem large but manageable. Look off the books, and a second, bigger pile of debt appears. A Nikkei study put that hidden figure at $1.65 trillion, up roughly eightfold in four years. It is more than the $1.35 trillion the five […]
content_html:
Look at what Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, and Oracle officially owe, and the numbers seem large but manageable. Look off the books, and a second, bigger pile of debt appears.

A Nikkei study put that hidden figure at $1.65 trillion, up roughly eightfold in four years. It is more than the $1.35 trillion the five report outright.

The Enron echo

The money is tied up in off-balance-sheet vehicles, the same kind of structure Enron used to hide debt before it collapsed 25 years ago. Back then it was fraud. Now, tightened rules and fuller disclosures make it legal.

The tools are still there, though. “Enron’s crime wasn’t having special purpose vehicles,” analyst Gil Luria told Bloomberg Law. “Enron’s crime was hiding them.”

How it works

The 💜 of EU tech

The latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. It's free, every week, in your inbox. Sign up now!

The mechanics are simple. A company packages the debt for chips, servers, and power into a separate legal entity, often a joint venture, so the cost never flows through its own accounts.

Take Meta’s Hyperion data centre in Louisiana. Meta and Blue Owl Capital both put equity into a separate structure that took on $27 billion in debt. Meta is the sole tenant, yet argues it does not have to record that debt, because it is not the one who must find replacement tenants.

Oracle has $260 billion of future lease commitments that will eventually land on its books. Nvidia carries $119 billion in purchase obligations. Alphabet and Microsoft keep their vehicles off-book too.

The numbers behind the boom

The scale is the story. Meta’s off-balance-sheet debt alone is about $420 billion, nearly triple its reported debt. Oracle’s has grown roughly thirtyfold in four years.

It is all in service of the same race. The industry is expected to spend more than $3 trillion through 2028 building and equipping AI data centres, much of it financed against the chips inside them.

Why it matters now

The timing is awkward. Four of the five report earnings in the next two weeks, and the reported debt will look tidy. The $1.65 trillion sitting in the footnotes will not make the headlines.

The catch is what happens later. When a data centre goes live, its lease rolls onto the balance sheet at once. If AI demand falls short, the facility is marked down, and the loss lands on the lenders and insurers who funded it.

Some already see the risk. S&P has cut Oracle’s credit rating over stretched leverage, and both Morgan Stanley and Moody’s have flagged the wider issue. “What if one of these companies was a house of cards,” asked accounting consultant Tom Selling, “and was propping itself up with this accounting treatment?”

The honest version

None of this is illegal, and the companies insist future earnings will more than cover the bills. The disclosures exist, buried in the footnotes, for anyone willing to dig.

But it means investors reading this week’s results are seeing less than half the leverage. In a year of loud bubble talk, the quiet number is the one hiding off the page.

blocks.html_en: The honest version

sentences.en: The honest version
sentences.zh: 诚实的版本

blocks.html_en: None of this is illegal, and the companies insist future earnings will more than cover the bills. The disclosures exist, buried in the footnotes, for anyone willing to dig.

sentences.en: None of this is illegal, and the companies insist future earnings will more than cover the bills.
sentences.zh: 这些都不违法，而且这些公司坚称未来的收益足以支付账单。

sentences.en: The disclosures exist, buried in the footnotes, for anyone willing to dig.
sentences.zh: 这些披露存在于脚注中，供任何愿意挖掘的人使用。

blocks.html_en: But it means investors reading this week’s results are seeing less than half the leverage. In a year of loud bubble talk, the quiet number is the one hiding off the page.

sentences.en: But it means investors reading this week’s results are seeing less than half the leverage.
sentences.zh: 但这意味着阅读本周业绩的投资者看到的杠杆率还不到一半。

sentences.en: In a year of loud bubble talk, the quiet number is the one hiding off the page.
sentences.zh: 在泡沫言论喧嚣的一年中，安静的数字是隐藏在页面之外的数字。

content_html_zh:
看看 Alphabet、微软、亚马逊、Meta 和 Oracle 的官方欠款，数字看起来很大，但在可控范围内。 撇开账簿，第二笔更大的债务就会出现。

日经指数的一项研究表明，这个隐藏数字为 1.65 万亿美元，四年内增长了大约八倍。 这比五次报告的总金额 1.35 万亿美元还要多。

安然回声

这些资金被绑在表外工具上，这与安然公司 25 年前倒闭前用来隐藏债务的结构相同。 那时候就是诈骗。 现在，更严格的规则和更全面的披露使其合法化。

不过，工具仍然在那里。 “安然的犯罪行为并不是拥有特殊目的工具，”分析师吉尔·卢里亚（Gil Luria）告诉彭博社法律。 “安然的罪行就是隐藏了他们。”

它是如何运作的

欧盟科技的💜

来自欧盟科技界的最新传言、我们明智的创始人鲍里斯的故事，以及一些值得怀疑的人工智能艺术。 每周都会免费发送到您的收件箱中。 立即注册！

机制很简单。 公司将芯片、服务器和电力的债务打包到一个单独的法人实体（通常是合资企业）中，因此成本永远不会流经自己的账户。

以路易斯安那州 Meta 的 Hyperion 数据中心为例。 Meta 和 Blue Owl Capital 都将股权投入到一个单独的结构中，该结构承担了 270 亿美元的债务。 Meta 是唯一的租户，但它辩称它不必记录这笔债务，因为它不是必须寻找替代租户的人。

甲骨文有 2600 亿美元的未来租赁承诺，最终将记入其账簿。 Nvidia 承担了 1190 亿美元的采购义务。 Alphabet 和微软也将他们的车辆保留在账外。

繁荣背后的数字

规模就是故事。 仅 Meta 的表外债务就约为 4200 亿美元，几乎是其报告债务的三倍。 甲骨文的规模在四年内增长了大约三十倍。

这一切都是为同一个种族服务的。 到 2028 年，该行业预计将花费超过 3 万亿美元来建设和装备人工智能数据中心，其中大部分资金来自于数据中心内部的芯片。

为什么现在很重要

时机很尴尬。 五家公司中的四家将在未来两周报告收益，报告的债务看起来会很干净。 脚注中的 1.65 万亿美元不会成为头条新闻。

问题是稍后会发生什么。 当数据中心投入使用时，其租赁立即计入资产负债表。 如果人工智能需求不足，该贷款就会被打折，而损失则落在为其提供资金的贷方和保险公司身上。

有些人已经看到了风险。 标准普尔因杠杆率过高而下调了甲骨文的信用评级，摩根士丹利和穆迪都指出了更广泛的问题。 会计顾问汤姆·塞林 (Tom Selling) 问道：“如果其中一家公司是纸牌屋，并通过这种会计处理方式支撑自己，该怎么办？”

诚实的版本

这些都不违法，而且这些公司坚称未来的收益足以支付账单。 这些披露存在于脚注中，供任何愿意挖掘的人使用。

但这意味着阅读本周业绩的投资者看到的杠杆率还不到一半。 在泡沫言论喧嚣的一年中，安静的数字是隐藏在页面之外的数字。
